name: TypeScript Basics
description:
Test your knowledge of **TypeScript fundamentals** — type annotations, interfaces, unions, generics, and optional chaining.

All questions use real TypeScript code. Example:

```typescript
const greet = (name: string): string => `Hello, ${name}!`;
console.log(greet("TypeScript"));  // Hello, TypeScript!
```

choices.text: `age` is a nullable type — it can be `null`
`age` is an optional property — it may be omitted
Syntax error — `?` is not valid here

questions.explanation:
`?` marks a property as **optional** — the object can be created without providing it. The type of `age` becomes `number | undefined`:

```typescript
interface User {
    name: string;
    age?: number;  // type is number | undefined
}

const u1: User = { name: "Alice" };          // OK — age omitted
const u2: User = { name: "Bob", age: 30 };   // OK — age provided

console.log(u1.age);  // undefined
console.log(u2.age);  // 30
```

questions.text: Which correctly defines a **union type** for a variable that can hold either a `string` or a `number`?
questions.explanation:
`|` is the **union type operator** — it means the variable can be *either* type:

```typescript
let id: string | number;

id = 42;       // OK
id = "abc";    // OK
// id = true;  // Error: boolean is not string | number
```

`&` is the **intersection** operator (combines types, both must be satisfied). `||` and `Union<>` are not valid TypeScript type syntax.

choices.text: It returns the argument cast to `any`
It returns the argument with its exact type preserved
It converts any argument to a string

questions.text: Which of the following are **valid TypeScript type annotations**? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation:
TypeScript has no `int` type — the numeric type is `number`. All other options are valid:

```typescript
let x: string = "hello";           // string literal
let y: number[] = [1, 2, 3];       // array shorthand
let z: Array<boolean> = [true];    // generic Array syntax

// let w: int = 5;                 // Error: 'int' is not a type in TS
let w: number = 5;                 // correct
```

`number[]` and `Array<number>` are equivalent — both are valid.

`type Status = "active" | "inactive" | "pending"` is a **string literal union type** — a union of specific string values. TypeScript enforces at compile time that only these values are accepted:

```typescript
type Status = "active" | "inactive" | "pending";

describe("active");    // OK → "Status is: active"
describe("inactive");  // OK
// describe("deleted"); // Error: not assignable to type Status
```

Literal types are a powerful way to model state machines and enumerations with full type safety.
